title: The temptation of Jesus part 2
scripture_text: ‘’Then Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil After fasting forty days and forty nights, he was hungry The tempter came to him and said, “If you are the Son of God, tell these stones to become bread.” Jesus answered, “It is written: ‘Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of God.’ Then the devil took him to the holy city and had him stand on the highest point of the temple. “If you are the Son of God, he said, “throw yourself down. For it is written: ‘He will command his angels concerning you, and they will lift you up in their hands, so that you will not strike your foot against a stone.’ Jesus answered him, ‘’It is also written: ‘Do not put the Lord your God to the test.’ Again, the devil took him to a very high mountain and showed him all the kingdoms of the world and their splendour “All this I will give you,” he said, “if you will bow down and worship me.” Jesus said to him, “Away from me, Satan! For it is written: ‘Worship the Lord your God, and serve him only.’ Then the devil left him, and angels came and attended him.
devotional:
A requirement of son-ship is that you are continually under the influence and direction of the Holy Spirit.

Being a child of God means that you have a continual, developing need of the presence and work of the Spirit in your life. We fall into a trap of thinking that the work of the Spirit in our lives is for those times when I am ‘serving God,’ or ‘helping others.’ But no matter who or where we are, our need of the developing work of the Spirit in our hearts will never diminish or decrease. It is the work and ways of the Holy Spirit in us that distinguishes us as children of God.

We are told that the Spirit led Jesus into the wilderness. The word translated ‘sent’ in Mark 1:12 is a very weak translation. The word he uses for “Sent” is an extremely strong word, it literally means to drive out your enemies. In the LXX (the Septuagint which is the Greek translation of the Hebrew scriptures), the word is used in the context where an enemy, frustrating or standing in the way of God’s fulfilling his purpose for his chosen people, is ‘cast out’ by God, so that God’s purposes may be fulfilled. See Exodus 23:30 Deuteronomy 33:27ff.

The word was mostly used in the New Testament in relation to casting out demons. The word expresses passion of God’s Ekballo, or God’s resistance to evil. God was sending his son into the wilderness with a strong passion so he could confront and unmask the evil one.

It is interesting to note that the word Luke uses to describe the Spirit leading Jesus into the wilderness. The word almost means the complete opposite to Ekballo, it has the idea to carry, to bring, and to keep.

Protected by passion

So on the one hand we have the Spirit driving Jesus into the wilderness, as an expression of God passion against evil; and on the other had we have the keeping power of the Spirit leading Jesus. This is not a contradiction, nor is it even two opposites. God’s passion and God’s protection always go together.

This was so important for Jesus at this time and so encouraging.

Think of the lessons he was learning.

Spiritual warfare was God Initiative. Jesus was about to enter into a ministry which would be marked with confrontation with the powers of darkness. There would be many times when He would feel under pressure with his back against the wall and it was important that He knew that it was God initiating this with the purpose of exposing satan. Jesus would be secure in the knowledge that His Father was leading Him into Spiritual conflict, rather than being a victim of satans attack.

(It is interesting to note that in the four exorcisms that Mark covers in detail Jesus always goes to where the demon is, the demon doesn’t go to Him.)

The Father did not lead Jesus into Spiritual warfare until Jesus was assured of His fathers love. Feeling secure in the father’s love is an essential to coping with spiritual warfare. Because Jesus was feeling secure in his father’s love he was able to handle the temptations of Satan.

We need to make a comment about how some people understand the temptation of Jesus in light of his Baptism. Many people see the Temptation of Jesus as a reaction of Satan to what Jesus’ Father said to him when he was baptized. This in turn has lead to some people creating a “cause and effect theology” which says something like. “I must be doing something right because Satan is attacking me.” They believe that the blessing of God makes them more open to Satan’s attacks. This is both dangerous and inconsistent with scripture.

To think in these terms is to get your affirmation from Satan not from God.

The bible teaches that Spiritual warfare is not Satan reacting to God, but God pursuing Satan.
prayer: Father thank you that when you lead me into spiritual warfare, I can trust your love and protection.
html:
The temptation of Jesus part 2

Study Text: Matthew 4:1-11
‘’Then Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil After fasting forty days and forty nights, he was hungry The tempter came to him and said, “If you are the Son of God, tell these stones to become bread.” Jesus answered, “It is written: ‘Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of God.’ Then the devil took him to the holy city and had him stand on the highest point of the temple. “If you are the Son of God, he said, “throw yourself down. For it is written: ‘He will command his angels concerning you, and they will lift you up in their hands, so that you will not strike your foot against a stone.’ Jesus answered him, ‘’It is also written: ‘Do not put the Lord your God to the test.’ Again, the devil took him to a very high mountain and showed him all the kingdoms of the world and their splendour “All this I will give you,” he said, “if you will bow down and worship me.” Jesus said to him, “Away from me, Satan! For it is written: ‘Worship the Lord your God, and serve him only.’ Then the devil left him, and angels came and attended him.

A requirement of son-ship is that you are continually under the influence and direction of the Holy Spirit.

Being a child of God means that you have a continual, developing need of the presence and work of the Spirit in your life. We fall into a trap of thinking that the work of the Spirit in our lives is for those times when I am ‘serving God,’ or ‘helping others.’ But no matter who or where we are, our need of the developing work of the Spirit in our hearts will never diminish or decrease. It is the work and ways of the Holy Spirit in us that distinguishes us as children of God.

We are told that the Spirit led Jesus into the wilderness. The word translated ‘sent’ in Mark 1:12 is a very weak translation. The word he uses for “Sent” is an extremely strong word, it literally means to drive out your enemies. In the LXX (the Septuagint which is the Greek translation of the Hebrew scriptures), the word is used in the context where an enemy, frustrating or standing in the way of God’s fulfilling his purpose for his chosen people, is ‘cast out’ by God, so that God’s purposes may be fulfilled. See Exodus 23:30 Deuteronomy 33:27ff.

The word was mostly used in the New Testament in relation to casting out demons. The word expresses passion of God’s Ekballo, or God’s resistance to evil. God was sending his son into the wilderness with a strong passion so he could confront and unmask the evil one.

It is interesting to note that the word Luke uses to describe the Spirit leading Jesus into the wilderness. The word almost means the complete opposite to Ekballo, it has the idea to carry, to bring, and to keep.

So on the one hand we have the Spirit driving Jesus into the wilderness, as an expression of God passion against evil; and on the other had we have the keeping power of the Spirit leading Jesus. This is not a contradiction, nor is it even two opposites. God’s passion and God’s protection always go together.

This was so important for Jesus at this time and so encouraging.

Think of the lessons he was learning.

Spiritual warfare was God Initiative. Jesus was about to enter into a ministry which would be marked with confrontation with the powers of darkness. Jesus would be secure in the knowledge that His Father was leading Him into Spiritual conflict, rather than being a victim of satans attack.

(It is interesting to note that in the four exorcisms that Mark covers in detail Jesus always goes to where the demon is, the demon doesn’t go to Him.)

The Father did not lead Jesus into Spiritual warfare until Jesus was assured of His fathers love. Feeling secure in the father’s love is an essential to coping with spiritual warfare. Because Jesus was feeling secure in his father’s love he was able to handle the temptations of Satan.

We need to make a comment about how some people understand the temptation of Jesus in light of his Baptism. Many people see the Temptation of Jesus as a reaction of Satan to what Jesus’ Father said to him when he was baptized. This in turn has lead to some people creating a “cause and effect theology” which says something like. “I must be doing something right because Satan is attacking me.” They believe that the blessing of God makes them more open to Satan’s attacks. This is both dangerous and inconsistent with scripture.

To think in these terms is to get your affirmation from Satan not from God.

The bible teaches that Spiritual warfare is not Satan reacting to God, but God pursuing Satan.

Prayer: Father thank you that when you lead me into spiritual warfare, I can trust your love and protection.
Bible in a year reading plan: - 2 Samuel 11-13
